Description: Optimal average power for Oak surface cleaning without charring
Near-IR wavelength for optimal absorption in Oak cellulose and lignin
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning resolution
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without wood damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage
Recommended number of passes for complete surface cleaning
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning pattern
Optimal dwell time per cleaning spot

Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in wood cleaning to remove contaminants without charring; starting at 50 W for light soiling on oak, max limited by oak's low thermal conductivity (~0.17 W/m·K) to avoid substrate damage. Source: Studies on laser cleaning of wooden artifacts (e.g., Journal of Cultural Heritage, 2015).

Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning of oak; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., mold or grease) to ensure removal, faster for light dust; 500 mm/s optimal for balancing efficiency and heat control. Source: Industry manuals from IPG Photonics and Adapt Laser Systems.

Nanosecond regime preferred for industrial cleaning of oak to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <50 μm); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications; 20 ns balances ablation of contaminants with oak's sensitivity to thermal damage. Source: Laser physics research in Optics & Lasers in Engineering (2018).

Typical for 1064 nm cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for oak to achieve fast coverage without excessive heat accumulation, as higher rates (>50 kHz) risk charring due to oak's low reflectivity (~10-20% at 1064 nm). Trade-off: higher freq speeds process but increases thermal load. Source: Academic literature, Applied Surface Science (2020).

Fluence range for contaminant removal on wood; oak damage threshold ~3-5 J/cm² (ablation starts at ~1 J/cm² for organics); 1.5 J/cm² safe for cleaning without degrading cellulose structure. Conservative due to oak's melting/decomposition point ~200-300°C. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017).

Beam diameter for precise industrial cleaning; smaller for fine details on oak surfaces (e.g., engravings), larger for broad areas; 200 μm recommended for uniform contaminant removal without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf lasers.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers; 2 passes typical for oak to ensure complete removal while monitoring for discoloration. Source: Industry case studies on wood restoration (e.g., Laser Cleaning Conference proceedings, 2019).

Ensures uniform coverage in scan patterns; 50% recommended for oak to avoid gaps in cleaning while preventing over-exposure and thermal buildup. Source: Process optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2021).
